paper_base: Revised Paper - Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture with Dynamic Quantization for Real-Time Schizophrenia Screening

data.text:
Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture with
Dynamic Quantization for Real-Time
Schizophrenia Screening
Jayesh RL1, Rajath U1, Vinutha N1, Nagaraju Y2, and Sriramkumar R1
1 Department of CSE(AI & ML), Dayananda Sagar University, Bengaluru,
Karnataka, India
jayeshrl2005@gmail.com, rajathdsu@gmail.com, vinutha1v@gmail.com,
sriramkumar2686@gmail.com
2 Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Dayananda Sagar College of
Engineering, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India
nagarajuyat22@gmail.com
Abstract. Schizophrenia is a severe psychiatric condition impacting
roughly 24 million individuals globally, marked by impairments in per-
ceptual processing, cognitive function, and behavioral regulation. Early
and accurate diagnosis remains challenging due to the reliance on sub-
jective clinical assessments. Electroencephalography (EEG) offers a non-
invasive, cost-effective approach for capturing neural biomarkers associ-
ated with schizophrenia. In this paper, we propose an efficient deep learn-
ing framework utilizing quantized Spiking Separable Multiscale Convo-
lutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF)
neurons for automated schizophrenia detection from multi-channel EEG
signals. Our approach combines the temporal processing capabilities of
biological-inspired spiking neurons with model quantization techniques
to enable deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. We bench-
marked three different architectures on a publicly available data set of
84 adolescent subjects with 16, channel EEG recordings:
(i) Separable Multiscale CNN yielding 60.71% accuracy, (ii) Spiking Sep-
arable Multiscale CNN with LIF neurons, which reached 82.92% accu-
racy and 0.896 AUC, ROC, and (iii) Quantized Spiking Separable Mul-
tiscale CNN holding the same level of accuracy 82.92% but the model
size was reduced by 1.30× (from 201 KB to 154 KB) by applying Py-
Torch dynamic int8 quantization. The high sensitivity of 82.43% and
specificity of 83.46% demonstrate clinical applicability for schizophrenia
screening, while the compact model size enables real-time deployment on
edge devices for point-of-care diagnostics.
Keywords: Deep Learning, Edge Computing, Electroencephalography,
Model Quantization, Schizophrenia Detection, Spiking Neural Networks
1
Introduction
Schizophrenia is a severe and chronic mental disorder that profoundly impacts
an individual’s ability to think, feel, and behave clearly. According to the World

2
Jayesh RL et al.
Health Organization (WHO), schizophrenia affects approximately 1 in 300 peo-
ple (0.32%) worldwide, with around 24 million people currently diagnosed with
this condition globally [1]. Usually, a person with this disorder would show symp-
toms in the years of late adolescence or early adulthood. The disorder is charac-
terized by the presence of symptoms like hallucinations, delusions, disorganized
speech, and neurocognitive deficits. If treatment is not given in time, the disor-
der is likely to result in a patient being severely disabled, having a diminished
quality of life, and even a higher risk of death.
Nowadays, identifying schizophrenia mainly relies on clinical interviews, be-
havioral observation, and standardized psychiatric assessment tools such as the
Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS) and the Diagnostic and Statis-
tical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM, 5) criteria.
Nevertheless, these methods are highly subjective, lengthy, and require a
highly competent psychiatrist. Moreover, the lack of objective biomarkers usually
leads to patients being diagnosed at a later stage, as the duration of untreated
psychosis is on average one to two years.
Neuroimaging modalities are widely regarded as potential avenues for ob-
jectively diagnosing schizophrenia. Different approaches have been investigated,
such as functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI), Positron Emission To-
mography (PET), Magnetoencephalography (MEG), and Electroencephalogra-
phy (EEG). Whereas fMRI and PET give us a very accurate localization of brain
functions, they are costly, require elaborate installations, and also entail the ad-
ministration of radioactive substances or contrast agents. On the other hand,
EEG has a number of unique benefits when it comes to clinical applications: the
method is non-invasive, relatively cheap, highly portable, offers immediate real-
time (millisecond-level) temporal resolution, and can be conducted quite easily
in a variety of clinical settings. These characteristics make EEG particularly
suitable for developing accessible screening tools in resource-limited healthcare
environments.
Recent deep learning technological revolutions have displayed astounding
results in the automated analysis of EEG for the detection of various brain
disorders. Rahul et al. [2] have offered a thorough systematic review of EEG,
based automated schizophrenia classification using machine learning and deep
learning, pointing out the increasing significance of AI tools in mental health
diagnosis. Chen [3] fashioned a ten, layer CNN model for schizophrenia classifi-
cation that utilized multichannel EEG which led to a stunning mean efficiency
of 99.18%. Latreche et al. [4] experimented with a gated recurrent unit (GRU),
based model particularly on alpha, EEG rhythms, resulting in a performance
of 88.88%. Srisatjaluk et al. [5] have suggested a new schizophrenia diagnosis
method using multimodal data and classification through EEGNet architecture,
revealing the effectiveness of linking electrophysiological data with the com-
plementary behavioral, affective signals. Moreover, it has been experimentally
confirmed by Zhang and Li [6] that among EEG, based applications, spiking
architectures featuring attention mechanisms are the best match. Spiking neu-
ral networks (SNNs), which are biologically inspired by the working of human

Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection
3
nerves, can conjugate potentially more energy, efficient usage with better tempo-
ral data handling, thus, they are looked upon as a promising model [7]. Besides
that, standard model quantization methods can result in a major reduction of
model size by lowering the precision of the representation such as the change
from 32, bit floating point to 8, bit integer, without causing a major drop in the
performance of the model [8].
The important contributions of this paper are as follows:
1. We develop a spiking neural network architecture with LIF neuron dynamics
optimized for multi-channel EEG-based schizophrenia classification, achiev-
ing 82.92% accuracy and 0.896 AUC-ROC on a publicly available dataset.
2. We implement PyTorch dynamic int8 post-training quantization, achieving
1.30× model compression (201 KB to 154 KB) while preserving classification
accuracy for edge deployment.
3. We provide comprehensive comparison of three architectures with varying
complexity-accuracy trade-offs, along with detailed clinical performance met-
rics including sensitivity (82.43%) and specificity (83.46%).
The rest of the paper is organized as follows:
Section II presents the related work on EEG, based schizophrenia detection
and neural network quantization.Section III details our proposed methodology
covering dataset, preprocessing, model architectures, and quantization approach.
Section IV illustrates experimental results and discussion. Finally, Section V
wraps up the paper by pointing out future research directions.
2
Literature Review
2.1
EEG-Based Schizophrenia Detection
Deep learning has advanced automated EEG analysis significantly. Chen [3]
achieved 99.18% accuracy using a ten-layered CNN, while Latreche et al. [4]
demonstrated GRU-based models for EEG rhythm patterns. Srisatjaluk et al.
[5] introduced multimodal EEGNet frameworks. Zhang and Li [6] validated spik-
ing networks for neurological signal processing.
2.2
Efficient Neural Network Architectures
Depthwise separable convolutions [10] reduce computational complexity by fac-
torizing convolutions. Spiking neural networks encode information via discrete
spike timing rather than continuous activations [7], enabling energy-efficient neu-
romorphic implementations.
2.3
Model Quantization
Quantization is one of the main methods to compress deep neural networks. It
does so by lowering the bit-width of weights and activations. Jacob et al. [8]

4
Jayesh RL et al.
have shown that 8-bit quantization was able to deliver results with accuracy
that was practically the same as that of full-precision models, and at the same
time, the size of the model was reduced by 4×. Through proper calibration, the
quantization error can be greatly reduced, thus accuracy can still be maintained.
3
Methodology
This part is about the framework we propose for automated schizophrenia di-
agnosis from EEG signals. Here, we use the data specified in Table 1 for the
training and testing of a Spiking Separable Multiscale CNN. The general struc-
ture of this network is shown in Fig. 1. The model mainly uses efficient separable
convolutions, mathematically expressed in Eq. (1), and spiking neurons to ex-
tract complex temporal patterns while maintaining low computational usage.
3.1
Dataset Description
We have adopted this dataset which is available publicly, EEG Schizophrenia
dataset, collected at Moscow State University by Borisov et al. [9]. In this
dataset, 16-channel EEG based recordings of 84 adolescents (39 normal con-
trols and 45 schizophrenia patients) sampled at 128 Hz for 60 s are present.
Table 1 presents a summary of the dataset specifications.
Ethical Considerations: This study uses a publicly available, fully anonymized
EEG dataset collected with informed consent under institutional ethical ap-
proval, as reported in the original dataset publication [9]. No new data were
collected, and no personally identifiable information was accessed. Therefore,
this study did not require additional ethical clearance.
Table 1. Dataset Specifications
Parameter
Value
Subjects
84
(39
Healthy,
45
Schizophrenia)
EEG Channels
16 (10–20 system)
Sampling Rate
128 Hz
Recording Length
60 s per subject
Samples per Channel
7,680
Electrodes
F7, F3, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz,
C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6,
O1, O2
3.2
Data Preprocessing
Our preprocessing workflow transforms raw EEG recordings into deep learning-
compatible representations while preserving clinically relevant neurophysiologi-
cal features. The steps in preprocessing include:

Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection
5
Signal Filtering and Artifact Rejection: Bandpass filtering (0.5–50 Hz)
isolates brain rhythms while removing high-frequency noise and low-frequency
drift. Automatic artifact detection removes segments affected by ocular move-
ment, muscle activity, or electrode artifacts.
Sliding Window Segmentation: Each 60-second recording is divided using
a 3-second window (384 samples at 128 Hz) with 75% overlap, yielding approx-
imately 97 windows per subject (8,160 total windows for 84 subjects). All 16
channels are retained to preserve spatial distribution of brain activity.
Normalization: We carry out StandardScaler normalization (µ = 0, σ = 1)
for each window separately and this happens inside every cross-validation fold
so that there wouldn’t be any data leakage.
Cross-Validation Strategy: A 5, Fold Group K, Fold strategy is used,
where the key point is that all windows from one subject should be in either the
training or validation set only to avoid data leakage and help perform a proper
generalization assessment across the subjects.
3.3
Proposed Model Architecture
Separable Multiscale CNN The first architecture employs depthwise sep-
arable convolutions [10] for parameter-efficient feature extraction from multi-
channel EEG data. The network consists of two parallel branches with different
kernel sizes (3 and 5) to capture multiscale temporal patterns in the EEG signal.
The separable convolution operation factorizes a standard convolution into:
SeparableConv(x) = PointwiseConv(DepthwiseConv(x))
(1)
where DepthwiseConv applies one filter to each input channel only, and Point-
wiseConv mixes outputs with 1×1 convolutions. Such factorization diminishes
the count of parameters from k × din × dout to k × din + din × dout, where k
denotes the kernel size and din, dout represent the input and output dimensions
respectively.
Spiking Separable Multiscale CNN with LIF Neurons The second ar-
chitecture integrates Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons into the separa-
ble multiscale framework. The network consists of three separable convolutional
blocks with increasing filter sizes (32, 64, 128), each followed by batch normal-
ization and LIF neuron layers. The LIF neuron dynamics are governed by Eq.
(2) and Eq. (3):
Vt = βVt−1 + It −St−1Vth
(2)
St =
(
1,
if Vt ≥Vth
0,
otherwise
(3)
where Vt represents the membrane potential at time step t, β = 0.9 is the
leak constant controlling the decay rate of the membrane potential, It is the

6
Jayesh RL et al.
Fig. 1. Top: Input multi-channel EEG signal representation. Bottom: Spiking Sepa-
rable Multiscale CNN architecture with parallel multiscale branches processing 16-
channel EEG inputs.

Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection
7
input current from the previous layer, Vth = 1.0 is the firing threshold, and St is
the binary spike output. When the membrane potential exceeds the threshold,
the neuron fires (St = 1) and the membrane potential is reset by subtracting
Vth. A fast sigmoid surrogate gradient (α = 25.0) is used for backpropagation
through the non-differentiable spiking function. The network processes inputs
across T = 20 time steps, and final classification is based on the average spike
rate over the simulation window.
Quantized Spiking Separable Multiscale CNN We apply PyTorch dy-
namic int8 post-training quantization to the trained Spiking CNN to reduce
model size and inference latency.
3.4
Quantization Methodology
We use PyTorch dynamic int8 quantization, which maps real-valued weights r
to 8-bit integers q using a scale S and zero-point Z via q = round(r/S) + Z, as
shown in Eq. (4).
q = round(r/S) + Z
(4)
3.5
Training Configuration
Each model is trained by Adam optimizer and binary cross, entropy loss. To
prevent overfitting, we apply Early stopping with patience of 10 epochs and
restoration of best weights. Table 2 shows the training hyperparameters used for
each model.
Table 2. Training Hyperparameters
Parameter
Value
Optimizer
Adam (lr=0.001)
Loss Function
Binary Cross-Entropy
Batch Size
128
Maximum Epochs
50
Early Stopping Patience
10 epochs
Validation Split
5-Fold Group K-Fold CV
4
Results and Discussion
4.1
Classification Performance
The table 3 presents the classification based performance comparison across all
three architectures evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation.
Our Spiking CNN architecture achieves 82.92% classification accuracy (vs.
60.71% baseline) with 0.896 AUC-ROC, validating the efficacy of spike-timing-
based temporal feature extraction for psychiatric EEG analysis.

8
Jayesh RL et al.
Table 3. Classification Performance Comparison
Model
Accuracy
F1
AUC
Prec.
Sep. Multiscale
60.71%
0.694
0.761
71.51%
Spiking CNN
82.92%
0.837
0.896
84.89%
Spiking CNN Q
82.92%
0.837
0.896
84.89%
4.2
Model Efficiency Analysis
The table 4 compares the computational efficiency of the three architectures in
terms of trainable parameters, model size, and compression ratio.
Table 4. Model Efficiency Comparison
Model
Params
Size
Compression
Sep. Multiscale
3,657
78 KB
1.0×
Spiking CNN
48,129
201 KB
1.0×
Spiking CNN Q
48,129
154 KB
1.30×
The Separable Multiscale CNN has the lowest number of parameters among
the models, with only 3, 657 trainable parameters and a model size of 78 KB.
The Spiking CNN has 48, 129 parameters, leading to a model size of 201 KB.
The model size of Spiking CNN is 2.6 × larger than that of the baseline. The use
of int8 quantization results in a significant reduction in the size of the Spiking
CNN to only 154 KB, which is a 1.30× compression ratio and still has the
same accuracy level. The very small model size (154 KB) of the Spiking CNN
makes it an excellent candidate for edge devices with memory constraints (e.g.,
microcontrollers, mobile devices, and embedded systems in point, of, care clinical
settings).
4.3
Clinical Performance Metrics
The Spiking CNN achieves sensitivity of 82.43% and specificity of 83.46%, with
precision of 84.89% and negative predictive value of 80.87%, ensuring balanced
clinical screening performance.
4.4
Quantization Impact and Cross-Validation Results
The table 5 summarizes the impact of int8 quantization on the Spiking CNN
model while also providing the detailed per-fold cross-validation results.
The quantized model achieves 1.30× compression while preserving accuracy
and reducing inference latency by 12%. Cross-validation shows stable perfor-
mance (CV = 6.2%), and the achieved 82.92% accuracy is competitive with
recent EEG-based schizophrenia detection methods.

Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection
9
Table 5. Quantization and Cross-Validation Summary
Metric
Float32
Int8
Accuracy
82.92%
82.92%
AUC
0.896
0.896
Model Size
201 KB
154 KB
Inference Time
99.7 ms
87.7 ms
Per-fold Accuracy (%)
Fold 1
86.34
Fold 2
75.25
Fold 3
87.87
Fold 4
79.66
Fold 5
85.48
4.5
Clinical Trade-off Analysis
Although prior CNN approaches report above 95% accuracy, such results of-
ten stem from sample-level data splitting that allows subject leakage between
train-test partitions, artificially inflating performance estimates. Our group-wise
cross-validation ensures subject-independent evaluation. This work prioritizes
deployment feasibility: the 154 KB quantized model enables edge deployment,
whereas heavy CNNs requiring GPU computation cannot serve point-of-care
settings.
4.6
Statistical Significance Analysis
To verify that the performance improvement of the proposed Spiking CNN is
not due to random variation, we conducted a one-sample t-test comparing the
5-fold cross-validation accuracies against the baseline Separable CNN accuracy
(60.71%). The t-statistic is computed as:
t = ¯x −µ0
s/√n
(5)
where as ¯x represents the mean fold accuracy (82.92%), µ0 is the baseline
accuracy (60.71%), s is the standard deviation of fold accuracies (5.14%), and n
is the number of folds (5).
Using the fold accuracies from Table 5 (86.34%, 75.25%, 87.87%, 79.66%,
85.48%), the calculated t-statistic is t ≈33.6 with p < 0.001, indicating that the
improvement achieved by the spiking architecture over the baseline is statistically
significant at the 0.001 significance level.
5
Conclusion
This paper presented an efficient deep learning based framework for EEG-based
schizophrenia detection utilizing quantized spiking neural networks with Leaky

10
Jayesh RL et al.
Integrate-and-Fire neuron dynamics. We developed and evaluated three archi-
tectures on a publicly available multi-channel EEG dataset of 84 adolescent sub-
jects, demonstrating that our Spiking CNN achieves 82.92% accuracy with 0.896
AUC-ROC and balanced clinical metrics of 82.43% sensitivity and 83.46% speci-
ficity. PyTorch dynamic int8 quantization reduces model size by 1.30× (201 KB
to 154 KB) while preserving accuracy and improving inference latency by 12%,
enabling edge deployment for point-of-care screening. Future work will explore
attention mechanisms, larger multi-site validation, and neuromorphic hardware
deployment.
References
1. World Health Organization, “Schizophrenia,” WHO Fact Sheets, 2022. [Online].
Available: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/schizophrenia
2. J. Rahul, D. Sharma, L. D. Sharma, U. Nanda, and A. K. Sarkar, ”A system-
atic review of EEG based automated schizophrenia classification through machine
learning and deep learning,” Front. Hum. Neurosci., vol. 18, p. 1347082, 2024.
3. H. Chen et al., ”Identification and diagnosis of schizophrenia based on multichannel
EEG and CNN deep learning model,” Schizophr. Res., vol. 270, pp. 44–56, 2024.
4. I. Latreche, S. Meshoul, and S. Harous, ”EEG-based schizophrenia diagnosis using
deep learning with multi-scale and adaptive feature selection,” PeerJ Comput. Sci.,
vol. 11, p. e2811, 2025.
5. R. Srisatjaluk, W. Mongkolpun, and K. Jenpanich, ”Diagnosis of schizophrenia
using multimodal data and classification using the EEGNet framework,” Intell.
Autom. Soft Comput., vol. 35, no. 2, pp. 1945–1962, 2024.
6. W. Zhang and P. Li, ”Spiking self-attention and wavelet transform for EEG-based
emotion recognition,” Front. Neurosci., vol. 18, pp. 135–147, 2025.
7. K. Roy, A. Jaiswal, and P. Panda, ”Towards spike-based machine intelligence with
neuromorphic computing,” Nature, vol. 575, no. 7784, pp. 607–617, 2019.
8. B. Jacob et al., ”Quantization and training of neural networks for efficient integer-
arithmetic-only inference,” in Proc. IEEE Conf. on Computer Vision and Pattern
Recognition (CVPR), 2018, pp. 2704–2713.
9. S. V. Borisov, A. Ya. Kaplan, N. L. Gorbachevskaya, and I. A. Kozlova, ”Analysis
of EEG structural synchrony in adolescents with schizophrenic disorders,” Hum.
Physiol., vol. 31, no. 3, pp. 255–261, 2005.
10. F. Chollet, ”Xception: Deep learning with depthwise separable convolutions,” in
Proc. IEEE Conf. on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2017,
pp. 1251–1258.
11. D. Anand, ”A Hybrid Spiking Convolutional Neural Network for Computationally
Efficient Pathology Image Classification,” in Proc. IEEE INSPECT, 2025, pp. 1–6.
12. C. S. S. K. Kaushik, V. N, and J. V. V, ”Hybrid Spiking Neural Network-Attention
Model for Multi-Class Heart Disease Detection,” in Proc. 6th Int. Conf. on Recent
Advances in Information Technology (RAIT), Dhanbad, India, 2025, pp. 1–6.

Schizophrenia is a severe psychiatric condition impacting roughly 24 million individuals globally, marked by impairments in per- ceptual processing, cognitive function, and behavioral regulation. Early and accurate diagnosis remains challenging due to the reliance on sub- jective clinical assessments. Electroencephalography (EEG) offers a non- invasive, cost-effective approach for capturing neural biomarkers associ- ated with schizophrenia. In this paper, we propose an efficient deep learn- ing framework utilizing quantized Spiking Separable Multiscale Convo- lutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons for automated schizophrenia detection from multi-channel EEG signals. Our approach combines the temporal processing capabilities of biological-inspired spiking neurons with model quantization techniques to enable deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. We bench- marked three different architectures on a publicly available data set of 84 adolescent subjects with 16, channel EEG recordings: (i) Separable Multiscale CNN yielding 60.71% accuracy, (ii) Spiking Sep- arable Multiscale CNN with LIF neurons, which reached 82.92% accu- racy and 0.896 AUC, ROC, and (iii) Quantized Spiking Separable Mul- tiscale CNN holding the same level of accuracy 82.92% but the model size was reduced by 1.30× (from 201 KB to 154 KB) by applying Py- Torch dynamic int8 quantization. The high sensitivity of 82.43% and specificity of 83.46% demonstrate clinical applicability for schizophrenia screening, while the compact model size enables real-time deployment on edge devices for point-of-care diagnostics. It does so by lowering the bit-width of weights and activations. Jacob et al. The model mainly uses efficient separable convolutions, mathematically expressed in Eq. (1), and spiking neurons to ex- tract complex temporal patterns while maintaining low computational usage. 3.1 Dataset Description We have adopted this dataset which is available publicly, EEG Schizophrenia dataset, collected at Moscow State University by Borisov et al. [9]. In this dataset, 16-channel EEG based recordings of 84 adolescents (39 normal con- trols and 45 schizophrenia patients) sampled at 128 Hz for 60 s are present. Table 1 presents a summary of the dataset specifications. Ethical Considerations: This study uses a publicly available, fully anonymized EEG dataset collected with informed consent under institutional ethical ap- proval, as reported in the original dataset publication [9]. No new data were collected, and no personally identifiable information was accessed. Therefore, this study did not require additional ethical clearance. Table 1. Dataset Specifications Parameter Value Subjects 84 (39 Healthy, 45 Schizophrenia)

al. [9]. In this dataset, 16-channel EEG based recordings of 84 adolescents (39 normal con- trols and 45 schizophrenia patients) sampled at 128 Hz for 60 s are present. Table 1 presents a summary of the dataset specifications. Ethical Considerations: This study uses a publicly available, fully anonymized EEG dataset collected with informed consent under institutional ethical ap- proval, as reported in the original dataset publication [9]. No new data were collected, and no personally identifiable information was accessed. Therefore, this study did not require additional ethical clearance. Table 1. Dataset Specifications Parameter Value Subjects 84 (39 Healthy, 45 Schizophrenia) EEG Channels 16 (10–20 system) Sampling Rate 128 Hz Recording Length 60 s per subject Samples per Channel 7,680 Electrodes F7, F3, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, O2 3.2 Data Preprocessing Our preprocessing workflow transforms raw EEG recordings into deep learning- compatible representations while preserving clinically relevant neurophysiologi- cal features. The steps in preprocessing include: Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection 5 Signal Filtering and Artifact Rejection: Bandpass filtering (0.5–50 Hz) isolates brain rhythms while removing high-frequency noise and low-frequency drift. Automatic artifact detection removes segments affected by ocular move- ment, muscle activity, or electrode artifacts. Sliding Window Segmentation: Each 60-second recording is divided using a 3-second window (384 samples at 128 Hz) with 75% overlap, yielding approx- imately 97 windows per subject (8,160 total windows for 84 subjects). All 16 channels are retained to preserve spatial distribution of brain activity. Normalization: We carry out StandardScaler normalization (µ = 0, σ = 1) for each window separately and this happens inside every cross-validation fold so that there wouldn’t be any data leakage. Cross-Validation Strategy: A 5, Fold Group K, Fold strategy is used, where the key point is that all windows from one subject should be in either the training or validation set only to avoid data leakage and help perform a proper generalization assessment across the subjects. 3.3 Proposed Model Architecture Separable Multiscale CNN The first architecture employs depthwise sep- arable convolutions [10] for parameter-efficient feature extraction from multi- channel EEG data. The network consists of two parallel branches with different kernel sizes (3 and 5) to capture multiscale temporal patterns in the EEG signal. The separable convolution operation factorizes a standard convolution into: SeparableConv(x) = PointwiseConv(DepthwiseConv(x)) (1) where DepthwiseConv applies one filter to each input channel only, and Point- wiseConv mixes outputs with 1×1 convolutions. Such factorization diminishes the count of parameters from k × din × dout to k × din + din × dout, where k denotes the kernel size and din, dout represent the input and output dimensions respectively. Spiking Separable Multiscale CNN with LIF Neurons The second ar- chitecture integrates Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons into the separa- ble multiscale framework. The network consists of three separable convolutional blocks with increasing filter sizes (32, 64, 128), each followed by batch normal- ization and LIF neuron layers. The LIF neuron dynamics are governed by Eq. (2) and Eq. (3): Vt = βVt−1 + It −St−1Vth (2) St = ( 1, if Vt ≥Vth 0, otherwise (3) where Vt represents the membrane potential at time step t, β = 0.9 is the leak constant controlling the decay rate of the membrane potential, It is the 6 Jayesh RL et al. Fig. 1. Top: Input multi-channel EEG signal representation. Bottom: Spiking Sepa- rable Multiscale CNN architecture with parallel multiscale branches processing 16- channel EEG inputs. Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection 7 input current from the previous layer, Vth = 1.0 is the firing threshold, and St is the binary spike output. When the membrane potential exceeds the threshold, the neuron fires (St = 1) and the membrane potential is reset by subtracting Vth. A fast sigmoid surrogate gradient (α = 25.0) is used for backpropagation through the non-differentiable spiking function. The network processes inputs across T = 20 time steps, and final classification is based on the average spike rate over the simulation window. Quantized Spiking Separable Multiscale CNN We apply PyTorch dy- namic int8 post-training quantization to the trained Spiking CNN to reduce model size and inference latency. 3.4 Quantization Methodology We use PyTorch dynamic int8 quantization, which maps real-valued weights r to 8-bit integers q using a scale S and zero-point Z via q = round(r/S) + Z, as shown in Eq. (4). q = round(r/S) + Z (4) 3.5 Training Configuration Each model is trained by Adam optimizer and binary cross, entropy loss. To

20 time steps, and final classification is based on the average spike rate over the simulation window. Quantized Spiking Separable Multiscale CNN We apply PyTorch dy- namic int8 post-training quantization to the trained Spiking CNN to reduce model size and inference latency. 3.4 Quantization Methodology We use PyTorch dynamic int8 quantization, which maps real-valued weights r to 8-bit integers q using a scale S and zero-point Z via q = round(r/S) + Z, as shown in Eq. (4). q = round(r/S) + Z (4) 3.5 Training Configuration Each model is trained by Adam optimizer and binary cross, entropy loss. To prevent overfitting, we apply Early stopping with patience of 10 epochs and restoration of best weights. Table 2 shows the training hyperparameters used for each model. Table 2. Training Hyperparameters Parameter Value Optimizer Adam (lr=0.001) Loss Function Binary Cross-Entropy Batch Size 128 Maximum Epochs 50 Early Stopping Patience 10 epochs Validation Split 5-Fold Group K-Fold CV 4 Results and Discussion 4.1 Classification Performance The table 3 presents the classification based performance comparison across all three architectures evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation. Our Spiking CNN architecture achieves 82.92% classification accuracy (vs. 60.71% baseline) with 0.896 AUC-ROC, validating the efficacy of spike-timing- based temporal feature extraction for psychiatric EEG analysis. 8 Jayesh RL et al. Table 3. Classification Performance Comparison Model Accuracy F1 AUC Prec. Sep. Multiscale 60.71% 0.694 0.761 71.51% Spiking CNN 82.92% 0.837 0.896 84.89% Spiking CNN Q 82.92% 0.837 0.896 84.89% 4.2 Model Efficiency Analysis The table 4 compares the computational efficiency of the three architectures in terms of trainable parameters, model size, and compression ratio. Table 4. Model Efficiency Comparison Model Params Size Compression Sep. Multiscale 3,657 78 KB 1.0× Spiking CNN 48,129 201 KB 1.0× Spiking CNN Q 48,129 154 KB 1.30× The Separable Multiscale CNN has the lowest number of parameters among the models, with only 3, 657 trainable parameters and a model size of 78 KB. The Spiking CNN has 48, 129 parameters, leading to a model size of 201 KB. The model size of Spiking CNN is 2.6 × larger than that of the baseline. The use of int8 quantization results in a significant reduction in the size of the Spiking CNN to only 154 KB, which is a 1.30× compression ratio and still has the same accuracy level. The very small model size (154 KB) of the Spiking CNN makes it an excellent candidate for edge devices with memory constraints (e.g., microcontrollers, mobile devices, and embedded systems in point, of, care clinical settings). 4.3 Clinical Performance Metrics The Spiking CNN achieves sensitivity of 82.43% and specificity of 83.46%, with precision of 84.89% and negative predictive value of 80.87%, ensuring balanced clinical screening performance. 4.4 Quantization Impact and Cross-Validation Results The table 5 summarizes the impact of int8 quantization on the Spiking CNN model while also providing the detailed per-fold cross-validation results. The quantized model achieves 1.30× compression while preserving accuracy and reducing inference latency by 12%. Cross-validation shows stable perfor- mance (CV = 6.2%), and the achieved 82.92% accuracy is competitive with recent EEG-based schizophrenia detection methods. Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture for Schizophrenia Detection 9 Table 5. Quantization and Cross-Validation Summary Metric Float32 Int8 Accuracy 82.92% 82.92% AUC 0.896 0.896 Model Size 201 KB 154 KB Inference Time 99.7 ms 87.7 ms Per-fold Accuracy (%) Fold 1 86.34 Fold 2 75.25 Fold 3 87.87 Fold 4 79.66 Fold 5 85.48 4.5 Clinical Trade-off Analysis Although prior CNN approaches report above 95% accuracy, such results of- ten stem from sample-level data splitting that allows subject leakage between train-test partitions, artificially inflating performance estimates. Our group-wise cross-validation ensures subject-independent evaluation. This work prioritizes deployment feasibility: the 154 KB quantized model enables edge deployment, whereas heavy CNNs requiring GPU computation cannot serve point-of-care settings. 4.6 Statistical Significance Analysis To verify that the performance improvement of the proposed Spiking CNN is not due to random variation, we conducted a one-sample t-test comparing the 5-fold cross-validation accuracies against the baseline Separable CNN accuracy (60.71%). The t-statistic is computed as: t = ¯x −µ0 s/√n (5) where as ¯x represents the mean fold accuracy (82.92%), µ0 is the baseline accuracy (60.71%), s is the standard deviation of fold accuracies (5.14%), and n is the number of folds (5). Using the fold accuracies from Table 5 (86.34%, 75.25%, 87.87%, 79.66%, 85.48%), the calculated t-statistic is t ≈33.6 with p < 0.001, indicating that the improvement achieved by the spiking architecture over the baseline is statistically significant at the 0.001 significance level.

The t-statistic is computed as: t = ¯x −µ0 s/√n (5) where as ¯x represents the mean fold accuracy (82.92%), µ0 is the baseline accuracy (60.71%), s is the standard deviation of fold accuracies (5.14%), and n is the number of folds (5). Using the fold accuracies from Table 5 (86.34%, 75.25%, 87.87%, 79.66%, 85.48%), the calculated t-statistic is t ≈33.6 with p < 0.001, indicating that the improvement achieved by the spiking architecture over the baseline is statistically significant at the 0.001 significance level. 5 Conclusion This paper presented an efficient deep learning based framework for EEG-based schizophrenia detection utilizing quantized spiking neural networks with Leaky 10 Jayesh RL et al. Integrate-and-Fire neuron dynamics. We developed and evaluated three archi- tectures on a publicly available multi-channel EEG dataset of 84 adolescent sub- jects, demonstrating that our Spiking CNN achieves 82.92% accuracy with 0.896 AUC-ROC and balanced clinical metrics of 82.43% sensitivity and 83.46% speci- ficity. PyTorch dynamic int8 quantization reduces model size by 1.30× (201 KB to 154 KB) while preserving accuracy and improving inference latency by 12%, enabling edge deployment for point-of-care screening. Future work will explore attention mechanisms, larger multi-site validation, and neuromorphic hardware deployment. References 1. World Health Organization, “Schizophrenia,” WHO Fact Sheets, 2022. data.paper_base: Revised Paper - Lightweight Spiking Neural Architecture with Dynamic Quantization for Real-Time Schizophrenia Screening

files.content:
import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset
from model import Simplemodel


def main():
    # Synthetic placeholder data — replace with actual dataset loader
    X = torch.randn(256, 128)
    y = torch.randint(0, 10, (256,))
    ds = TensorDataset(X, y)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)

    model = Simplemodel()
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
    loss_fn = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    model.train()
    for epoch in range(10):
        total_loss = 0.0
        for xb, yb in dl:
            logits = model(xb)
            loss = loss_fn(logits, yb)
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()
            total_loss += loss.item()
        print(f"Epoch {epoch + 1}/10 — Loss: {total_loss / len(dl):.4f}")
    print("Training complete.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
